title: ADJ Program Review 2020-21 (Part 1)
text:
Program Review 2020-2021: Administration of Justice (Review excludes Law
Enforcement Academy Courses: ADJ 2014, 2512, 2516, 2517)
Prepared by: Michele Korkowski
1. Description of Program:
The Administration of Justice (ADJ) program consists of an Associate of Science degree,
Associate of Science Degree for Transfer, and an 18-unit Certificate of Achievement (COA).
This program introduces students to the study of the criminal justice system in the United
States, and prepares students for careers in a variety of criminal justice fields at the local,
state, and federal levels.
The Administration of Justice Program also offers a comprehensive Public Safety Training
Program including a Law Enforcement Academy and State-mandated training courses for
the Law Enforcement Community.
a) Organization: The ADJ Program is under direction of the Dean of Career and
Technical Education. The ADJ faculty include one full-time Coordinator of the
Law Enforcement Academy, several adjunct faculty teaching in the Academy,
and one full-time faculty teaching the degree curriculum.
b) Primary purpose: The primary purpose of the program is to prepare students for
careers in criminal justice fields.
c) Whom you serve: We serve all students interested in criminal justice careers,
and provide curriculum to assist those already in the law enforcement fields to
obtain their degree.
d) Services provided: We offer curriculum leading to degrees and a Certificate of
Achievement, as well as a modular format Law Enforcement Academy. We also
offer student internships with local agencies through our Occupational Work
Experience Program.
e) How services are provided:
a. Degree applicable coursework
b. Transfer courses
c. Vocational courses
f) Describe how curriculum is up to date and needs based: Curriculum for the
degree and COA are updated every two years, as required. The most recent
updates were completed December 4, 2020. The curriculum is aligned with the
ADJ Programs of several north-state community colleges, and fulfills the needs
of those interested in careers in the criminal justice system.
g) Breakdown of classes offered within review cycle: For the degree and COA,
all ADJ courses are on a two-semester rotation. A student may begin the
program in the spring or the fall and complete all the required ADJ coursework
within two consecutive semesters. The course rotation is as follows:

title: ADJ Program Review 2020-21 (Part 2)
text:
2. External factors impacting program
a) Budgetary constraints or opportunities: None noted. The program has the
opportunity to grow online; a marketing plan was implemented in Fall 2020.
b) Competition from other colleges; direct competition from the larger population
areas with community colleges, such as Butte College and Shasta College.
c) University requirements: Our AS-T aligns with transfer to CSU; but the closest
CSU, Sacramento, has an impacted criminal justice program.
d) Institutional regulation, policies, mandates, etc. None noted.
e) Non-institutional regulations, polices, mandates, etc. None noted.
f) Job market
a. Requirements of employers: local law enforcement agencies have been
hiring our academy cadets who meet the minimum entry-level
requirements and have earned mandated certifications.
b. Developments in the field (current and former): New technologies,
especially in the area of laws and criminal investigations, require annual
curriculum updates.
g) Other: A lack of professional development funding requires faculty to maintain
currency in the field and learn about new technologies on their own. The lack of a
CBA also inhibits faculty from obtaining stipends to improve the program.
3. Progress on Outcomes Assessment
a) Summarize progress on SLO measures applied since last review: The
assessments used to measure student achievement in outcomes in the classes
listed below were all open-book exams. Overall, it appears that the students who
were not successful in achieving the outcomes were due to low reading

title: ADJ Program Review 2020-21 (Part 3)
text:
comprehension capabilities, lack of critical thinking ability, and poor time-
management skills.
• ADJ 1001: SLO Assessed: Analyze special topics in the criminal justice system such
as juvenile delinquency, drug policy, prison overcrowding, and cybercrime.
Assessment Instrument: Final Exam, Attempted: 12; Passed with 70% or higher: 9;
Reasoning: The students who did not successfully achieve this assessment had low
class participation rates. Since the exam was open-book, all students should have
been able to successfully achieve this assessment.
• ADJ 2002: SLO Assessed: Examine the historical development of law and the
philosophy of law. Assessment Instrument: Exam #1; Attempted: 4: Passed with
70% or higher: 3; Reasoning: The student who did not successfully achieve this
assessment had a very low class participation rate. Since the exam was open-book,
all students should have been able to successfully achieve this assessment.
• ADJ 1503: SLO Assessed: Evaluate the rules of evidence, including the exclusionary rule,
privileged communications, hearsay evidence and witness competency. Assessment
Instrument: Exam #2; Attempted: 13; Passed with 70% or higher: 13; Reasoning: All
students were successful in achieving this outcome.
• ADJ 1505: SLO Assessed: Understand the role of the investigator in the criminal
trial process, including testimony, evidence collection, preservation and
presentation, and the relationship of the investigator with witnesses and
professionals within the courtroom working group. Assessment Instrument: Final
Exam; Attempted: 13; Passed with a score of 70% or higher: 13; Reasoning: All
students were successful in achieving this outcome.
• ADJ 2526: SLO Assessed: Identify and describe the stages in the trial process.
Assessment Instrument: Exam #4; Attempted: 5; Passed with a score of 70% or
higher: 5. Reasoning: All students were successful in achieving this outcome.
b) Program, course, instructional improvements made as a result of outcomes
assessment: I will provide more assistance and referrals to students who are
unprepared to comprehend reading materials and critically analyze material.
c) Program assessment plan for next two years: Update curriculum and SLOs to
align with C-ID; Include new SLO assessments.
4. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators: Discuss program performance on each
program-specific data item provided by Research Office.
Course data provides insight to enrollments, which have declined over the 2017 – 2020 time
period. Importantly, the reduction in course offerings (academy and work experience) at the
San Francisco Police Department have significantly affected FTE’s in the ADJ program.
Additionally, ADJ 9079 is a course offered almost exclusively to SFPD officers for in-service
training. This course sees a 100% completion/success rate, and is offered to a much more
diverse (race and gender) community than that seen in Siskiyou County. This reduction in
FTE’s can be seen in the table below.

-- 3 of 6 --

title: ADJ Program Review 2020-21 (Part 4)
text:
a)
b) Course completion rate; set goal for future
a. Retention rates hover around 97%, but again, this data is skewed by the
100% retention rates seen in SFPD enrollments. However, if the data is
disaggregated by courses in the degree and certificate, we see very good
retention rates (85% - 98%). I suppose striving for 100% retention in all
courses is not outside of our reach.
c) Course success rate: set goal
a. Student success rates average 97%, with year to year rates hovering
between 96% and 98%. There were changes to the modality of courses
taught, as well as a change in instructors for different course that may also
have affected student success. Probably the best measure of success,
because of the noise in the data from aforementioned issues, will be the SLO
assessments for individual courses taught by individual instructors.
d) FT/PT faculty ratio – how it impacts program and student success
a. On the degree side, there is only one full time faculty member teaching a full
load of online courses. There is no ratio to measure the impacts of having
only one faculty member on the degree side.
5. Other Unit-specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research:
a) List of measures not provided in previous questions that you use to gauge program
effectiveness (transfers, degrees, certs, equity, etc.). Associate Degrees for Transfer
increased from 2 in 2017, to 7, in 2020. However, AA Degrees declined from 30 in
2017, to 13 in 2020. This may be due to an overall decline in enrollments.
b) Summary of results of these measures. The ADJ program has seen a decline in
enrollments since 2017, which may account for the lower number of AA degrees
awarded.
c) What did you learn from evaluation of these measures; what improvements have you
implemented or intend to implement as a result of this analysis. Student retention
and gender data are inconclusive due to missing data over classes and terms.
Demographic data are skewed by SFPD enrollments.
6. Evaluation: Based upon Question 1 and Q 2 – 5, provide analysis on what is going well
and why, and what is not going well and why. Overall, enrollments have dropped
significantly, with the steepest decline in Fall 2020, most likely due to COVID. The program
was already being updated and I was prepared to teach my load fully online, as this was the
purpose of the CVC-OEI Improving Online CTE Pathways grant work. I had completed
several professional development courses to implement best practices in online teaching

title: ADJ Program Review 2020-21 (Part 5)
text:

criminal investigations class. A marketing plan targeting those currently in high school or
working in law enforcement was implemented, but again, COVID may have affected
enrollments. Spring 2021 semester is already seeing an increase in enrollments, so perhaps

a) Representativeness of population served:
a. Race: Data collected on race is skewed by the course offerings at the San
Francisco Police Department. Data must be disaggregated by course in order
to determine our representative student population locally. For example, our
ADJ 9079 course is offered almost exclusively to currently employed officers
at SFPD.
b. Gender: Data collected on race is skewed by the course offerings at the San
Francisco Police Department. Data must be disaggregated by course in order to
determine our representative student population locally. For example, our ADJ
9079 course is offered almost exclusively to currently employed officers at SFPD.
b) Alternative modes and schedules of course delivery (online, evening, etc.)
a. The CVC-OEI Improving Online CTE Pathways grant funded development of
all ADJ degrees and Certificate of Achievement courses to be offered fully
online.
c) Partnerships (internal/external)
a. We’ve partnered with area high schools to offer their students the opportunity
to take our ADJ online courses while they are still in high school.

title: ADJ Program Review 2020-21 (Part 6)
text:
a. Development of a fully online ADJ program has utilized the best practices in
online course delivery by certified online instructor. Forensic kits were utilized
for at-home activities in the criminal investigations class.
e) Efficiency in operations
a. A two-semester, one year rotation of all ADJ degree and Certificate
coursework has been established.
f) Efficiency in resource use
g) Staffing
a. Only one full-time faculty member teaches fully online on the degree side.
Additional instructors with online teaching certifications should be recruited to
grow the online offerings. Additional instructors should be recruited to cover
face-to-face teaching responsibilities.
h) Participation in shared governance (within unit)
a. ADJ Instructor Korkowski is the current CTE Faculty Liaison, attending CTE
Advisory Committee meetings and reporting back to the Academic Senate.
i) Professional development and training
a. ADJ Instructor Korkowski has successfully completed the @One course
Humanizing Online Teaching and Learning.
j) Compliance with mandates
a. None noted for degree side.
7. Vision. What is your unit’s vision? Where would you like your program to be in four years?
Growth, buildings, changes, etc..
I would like to see enrollments increase. A marketing plan has been implemented to
promote the ADJ program, however, without more instructors, we cannot offer more courses
or sections. Online enrollment growth, especially in our remote geographical area, is
imperative for the survival of our degree and certificate program.
8. Progress on prior goals.
9. Four year action plan and CQIP. Summarize and reflect. Assign priority to each
goal/objective.
A four year plan consists of:
1. Recruiting and training new instructors
2. Marketing to law enforcement agencies and local high schools
3. Adding more courses and course sections as enrollments increase
10. CQIP form (not included - not requesting resources above and beyond normal annual
budget allocations.)
